statement: Claude Code's terminal CLI and the Desktop app 'Code' tab are the same engine reading the same config (CLAUDE.md, MCP, hooks, skills, settings).
The Desktop 'Code' tab is distinct from the legacy Claude desktop chat app, whose MCP config (claude_desktop_config.json) is separate and does not appear in the Code tab.
Headless / non-interactive mode (-p/--print, scripting, CI) is a terminal-CLI capability; the Desktop GUI is interactive only.
Claude Cowork is agentic non-coding knowledge work that shows its plan before acting; available on paid plans including Team and Enterprise.

Computer use and phone Dispatch are a Pro/Max research preview (macOS and Windows); the docs do NOT document Team/Enterprise exclusion, so 'not on Team' is an inference, restated as 'not part of the documented Team plan'.
Claude Code is included on every Team seat (Standard and Premium); Premium only buys more usage and is NOT the access gate. (A Premium/Code-seat requirement applies only to legacy Enterprise.)
Settings precedence, highest to lowest: managed (org) > command-line args > local project (.claude/settings.local.json) > shared project (.claude/settings.json) > user (~/.claude/settings.json).
Managed (enterprise-policy) settings sit at the top of precedence and cannot be overridden by a user, CLI flag, or project file.

The managed-settings keys strictKnownMarketplaces, blockedMarketplaces, strictPluginOnlyCustomization, allowedMcpServers, wslInheritsWindowsSettings, and minimumVersion all exist and are enforced (plus allowManagedHooksOnly / allowManagedMcpServersOnly / allowManagedPermissionRulesOnly).
A committed .claude/ directory travels with the repo: cloning it (and accepting the trust dialog) gives a teammate the project skills, agents, hooks, and settings.json.
OpenAI Codex is a separate coding agent with its own config tree (~/.codex); nothing syncs automatically between Codex and Claude Code.
Codex reads AGENTS.md for project instructions; Claude Code natively reads CLAUDE.md, NOT AGENTS.md, so teams bridge the two (symlink or generated mirror).
AGENTS.md is an open standard stewarded by the Agentic AI Foundation (Linux Foundation), used by ~60k projects and 20+ tools.

Agent Skills use the open SKILL.md format supported by both Claude Code and Codex, but each tool reads only its OWN skills directory (Claude .claude/skills, Codex .agents/skills); portability = author once, mirror into each tool's dir.
A Claude Code plugin can bundle slash commands, subagents, skills, hooks, MCP servers, and LSP servers into one installable unit.

A plugin marketplace is a catalog repo defined by .claude-plugin/marketplace.json; teams add it once (/plugin marketplace add), install plugins (/plugin install), and pull updates (/plugin marketplace update).
Skills are model-invoked via their description (progressive disclosure: discovery loads name+description, activation loads SKILL.md, bundled references load only when accessed).

Hooks fire deterministically on event+matcher conditions, independent of the model's choice, and can hard-block (exit 2 / deny); handlers can be shell commands, HTTP endpoints, MCP tool calls, LLM prompts, or subagents, across many events beyond PreToolUse/PostToolUse.
Each subagent runs in its own context window with a custom system prompt, restricted (least-privilege) tool access, and returns only a summary to the main context.

Installing a plugin activates its skills, hooks, and MCP servers as code under one trust decision; audits of open third-party marketplaces found malicious skills (Snyk: 76 confirmed malicious payloads among ~3,984 skills; 13.4% critical), and plugin-injection attacks are demonstrated.
For supplying always-true knowledge, a static docs-index (AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md) currently outperforms model-invoked Skills in published evals.

positions.name: passive-context-wins-now
positions.claim: Vercel eval: AGENTS.md docs-index 100% vs Skills 53-79% (skill never invoked in 56% of cases) - results matter now.

positions.name: skills-for-actions
positions.claim: Skills win for vertical, explicitly-triggered action workflows; the gap is a current model tool-use limitation that will close.

statement: Loading many MCP tools consumes a large share of the context window before work begins; the open question is whether to restrict tools or compress outputs.

positions.name: thin-gateway-cli-first
positions.claim: An MCP should be a thin secure gateway with a few high-level tools; migrate stateless tools (Jira/GitHub/AWS) to CLIs, reserve MCP for stateful (Playwright).

positions.name: compress-dont-restrict
positions.claim: Keep tool richness; route outputs through a compression middleware for ~98% context reduction.

statement: Claude Code vs Codex is not either/or; the productive pattern is to run both.

positions.name: claude-quality
positions.claim: Claude Code higher quality (~67% blind win), leads SWE-bench, full MCP; but tighter usage limits.

positions.name: codex-efficiency
positions.claim: Codex cheaper (~4x fewer tokens), leads Terminal-Bench; 'Codex for keystroke, Claude Code for commits'.

statement: The dominant CLAUDE.md anti-pattern at scale is the over-stuffed, unowned file; keep it short (~<200 lines), give it an owner, review it like code, and use a root + per-package Context Cascade.

To make 'configure once' span Claude Code and Codex, keep one canonical config and project it: generate-or-symlink AGENTS.md from CLAUDE.md, and mirror skills into each tool's dir (a portable source like ~/.agents/skills symlinked into .claude/skills and Codex's surface), with a drift check that fails CI on divergence. This lets a dev switch between the two tools in VS Code and get identical rules.

Claude Code settings (permissions, hooks, env) live in .claude/settings.json (committed for the team) with personal overrides in .claude/settings.local.json; settings merge across scopes with the higher scope winning per key.

Shared team setup falls into four tiers by where each thing lives: project config in the repo (.claude/), cross-project tools in a plugin marketplace, org policy in managed settings, and personal config local to each user.

A team's shared library is one set of skills/agents/tools distributed via a plugin marketplace plus repo .claude/ + AGENTS.md, made portable across Claude Code and Codex — the assembled output of the four tiers.
